title: Enforce Secret Hygiene and Prevent Plaintext Secret Leaks
description: Implement controls to prevent plaintext secret values from leaking into version control or logs, and ensure GitHub token permissions are properly scoped. This includes pre-commit scanning, CI checks, and documentation of required secret scopes.
subtasks: Add a pre-commit hook (e.g., using gitleaks or git-secrets) to scan for base64-encoded secrets and common secret patterns in YAML, JSON, and env files. Block commits if secrets are detected.
Update CI pipeline to run gitleaks or kubesec scanning on all committed files and fail the build if secrets are found.
Add a validation script to ensure values-dev.yaml never contains actual secret values (only resource names or references).
If sealed-secrets is used, ensure only the encrypted manifest is committed and plaintext values are never present in the repo.
Document in the secret definition (sigma-1-secrets) the minimum required GitHub token permissions: contents: write, pull_requests: write, and scope the token to only the 5dlabs/sigma-1 repository.
Recommend using a GitHub App installation token or fine-grained PAT, not a classic PAT.
Run a test commit with a fake secret to verify the pre-commit hook and CI scanner block the commit/build.
Run gitleaks/gitsecrets/gitseekret scan on the current repository to ensure no secrets are present.
acceptance_criteria: Pre-commit hook blocks any commit containing secret values in YAML, JSON, or env files.
CI pipeline fails if a secret is detected in any committed file.
values-dev.yaml is validated to never contain actual secret values.
No plaintext secrets are present in the repository history (run gitleaks and confirm zero findings).
sigma-1-secrets manifest includes a comment specifying required GitHub token scopes.
A test commit with a fake secret is blocked by both pre-commit and CI.
If sealed-secrets is used, only encrypted manifests are present in the repo.

title: Restrict PM Server RBAC to Named Secrets Only
description: Update the sigma-1-pm-server ServiceAccount RBAC Role to restrict secret access to only the sigma-1-secrets resource using the resourceNames field, following least-privilege principles.
subtasks: Edit the Role for sigma-1-pm-server in sigma-1-dev namespace to use resourceNames: ['sigma-1-secrets'] for secrets.
If configmaps can be scoped, use resourceNames for configmaps as well (e.g., ['sigma-1-infra-endpoints']).
Update the RoleBinding as needed and re-apply manifests.
Run kubectl auth can-i get secrets/sigma-1-secrets --as=system:serviceaccount:sigma-1-dev:sigma-1-pm-server -n sigma-1-dev and verify access is allowed.
Run kubectl auth can-i get secrets/other-secret --as=system:serviceaccount:sigma-1-dev:sigma-1-pm-server -n sigma-1-dev and verify access is denied.
Document the RBAC policy in the repository.
acceptance_criteria: sigma-1-pm-server ServiceAccount can only get/list the sigma-1-secrets secret (not all secrets).
ConfigMap access is similarly scoped if possible.
kubectl auth can-i checks confirm least-privilege access.
RBAC manifests are updated and applied with no errors.
Documentation reflects the RBAC policy.

title: Implement Input Validation and Sanitization for PRD and API Endpoints
description: Add robust input validation and sanitization for all PRD input and API endpoints in the PM server pipeline to prevent injection attacks and resource exhaustion.
subtasks: Implement input validation on PRD input: enforce maximum length, allowed character set, and strip/escape markdown or HTML that could be used for injection.
Sanitize all task descriptions and user-supplied fields before embedding in Linear API calls, GitHub commit content, and Discord messages.
Add request body size limits to all PM server API endpoints (e.g., max 64KB per request).
Use a library such as zod or joi for schema validation of incoming requests.
Add unit tests for input validation and sanitization logic.
Run OWASP ZAP or similar dynamic scanner against the PM server API endpoints to verify no injection or resource exhaustion vectors are present.
acceptance_criteria: All PRD and API inputs are validated for length and character set.
Task descriptions and user-supplied fields are sanitized before being sent to external APIs.
API endpoints reject requests exceeding the configured size limit.
Unit tests cover all validation and sanitization logic.
OWASP ZAP scan reports no critical or high-severity findings related to input validation.

title: Implement Dependency Auditing, Pinning, and Container Image Scanning
description: Establish a supply chain security baseline by enforcing dependency version pinning, lockfile management, vulnerability auditing, and container image scanning for all services.
subtasks: Ensure bun.lockb and package-lock.json are committed and used in CI builds for backend and frontend.
Add a CI step to run bun audit or npm audit and fail the build on high/critical vulnerabilities.
Specify container base images in Dockerfiles using trusted registries and specific version tags (e.g., oven/bun:1.x-alpine, node:20-alpine), not latest.
Integrate Snyk, Trivy, or similar scanner in the CI pipeline to scan built container images for vulnerabilities.
Document the dependency and image scanning process in the repository.
Run a full dependency and image scan and remediate any high/critical findings.
acceptance_criteria: Lockfiles (bun.lockb, package-lock.json) are present and used in CI.
CI fails on high/critical vulnerabilities from bun audit/npm audit.
All Dockerfiles use pinned, trusted base images (no latest tags).
Container image scanning is part of the CI pipeline and blocks on high/critical findings.
Documentation exists for the supply chain security process.
A scan report shows no unaddressed high/critical vulnerabilities.

title: Harden Network Policies and Monitor PM Server Egress
description: Restrict PM server egress as much as possible using FQDN-based egress policies if supported, and implement monitoring and alerting for all egress traffic from PM server pods.
subtasks: If the CNI plugin supports FQDN-based egress (e.g., Cilium), restrict PM server egress to api.github.com, api.linear.app, and Hermes API domain.
If FQDN policies are not available, document the risk and allow only port 443 egress from PM server pods.
Configure logging of all egress traffic from PM server pods (e.g., via Cilium Hubble, Calico flow logs, or equivalent).
Set up alerts for unexpected egress destinations (e.g., any destination not in the allowlist).
Consider deploying an egress proxy for additional control and monitoring.
Test egress restrictions by attempting to connect to unauthorized external destinations from the PM server pod.
acceptance_criteria: NetworkPolicy restricts PM server egress to only required external APIs (FQDN-based if possible).
All egress traffic from PM server pods is logged.
Alerts are triggered on unexpected egress destinations.
Documentation exists for the egress policy and monitoring setup.
Test connections to unauthorized destinations are blocked and logged.

title: Implement Application-Level Security Event Logging
description: Add structured security event logging to the PM server for authentication, authorization, rate limiting, and external API calls. Integrate logs with the cluster's logging stack for alerting.
subtasks: Log all external API calls from the PM server, including destination, status code, and duration.
Log authentication and authorization events (API key usage, missing/invalid tokens, failed authorization checks).
Log rate-limit events and unusual request patterns.
Emit security events to a dedicated log stream or label them for easy filtering.
Integrate application logs with the cluster's logging stack (e.g., Fluentd, Loki, or ELK).
Set up alerting for suspicious or repeated failed authentication/authorization events.
Test logging by simulating failed authentication and rate-limit events and verifying they appear in the log aggregation system.
acceptance_criteria: All specified security events are logged in structured format.
Logs are integrated with the cluster's logging stack and can be queried.
Alerts are triggered on repeated failed authentication or authorization events.
Test events appear in the log aggregation system as expected.

title: Enforce Pod Security Standards and Harden Security Contexts
description: Configure pod security contexts for all deployments and enforce Kubernetes Pod Security Standards at the namespace level.
subtasks: Set securityContext.runAsNonRoot: true on all deployment containers.
Set securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem: true and use emptyDir volumes for temp data if needed.
Set securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation: false.
Set securityContext.capabilities.drop: [ALL].
Label the sigma-1-dev namespace with pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted.
Update Helm charts and manifests to include these securityContext fields.
Test that all pods start and function correctly with the new security contexts.
Run kubesec or kube-bench to verify compliance with Pod Security Standards.
acceptance_criteria: All pods run as non-root, with read-only root filesystem and no privilege escalation.
Pod Security Standards are enforced at the namespace level.
Pods start and function as expected with the new security contexts.
kubesec or kube-bench reports compliance with restricted Pod Security Standards.
